sources.short: Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #1580 Katherine Deming Cowles

narrative_blocks.header: Married her own fourth cousin Major Timothy Cowles
narrative_blocks.body: She married Major Timothy Cowles on 5 November 1809 — her own fourth cousin. Both descended from Rev Samuel Hooker through different sons, making this a documented cousin marriage uncommon enough to be flagged in family records.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father was the wealthiest man in Farmington
narrative_blocks.body: Catherine Deming's father Chauncey Deming was not only Farmington's wealthiest resident but a soldier of the Revolution — one half of the famous firm of John and Chauncey Deming, merchants. Catherine was his only surviving daughter and brought considerable wealth to her marriage with Major Timothy Cowles in 1809.

narrative_blocks.header: 28502 dollar estate inventory at her 1859 death
narrative_blocks.body: Her will was proved 29 November 1859 with an inventory of $28,502.66 — substantial Farmington wealth from her father Chauncey Deming's mercantile legacy. She had joined the church only in 1827 at age forty, late for a colonial-Republican Farmington matron.

cross_connections.display_label: Married her fourth cousin — both descend Rev. Samuel Hooker.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Deming' carries her father's surname into the next generation. Her grandson Chauncey Deming Cowles (H01838) likewise carried 'Deming' as middle name.

sources.short: Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #698 Major Timothy Cowles

notable.notable_blurb: Wealthy Farmington merchant who sheltered a Mende child

narrative_blocks.header: Built Farmington's stone store — and ran it
narrative_blocks.body: Major Timothy Cowles was a wealthy Farmington merchant whose home sat just south of the Congregational Church. He built the stone store destroyed by fire in 1864, owned the large hotel building later occupied by Porter Seminary, and served as state senator (1835), member of the Yale Corporation (1835) and liberal contributor to Yale's $100,000 fund, and president of Farmington Bank (1852). His estate at death was inventoried at $147,634.

narrative_blocks.header: Sheltered an Amistad survivor
narrative_blocks.body: When the three Mende girls who had survived the 1839 Amistad rebellion were brought to Farmington in 1841, the Cowles family took in one of them — believed by NPS to be Kagne. She would have attended school five hours each morning and shared in the family's domestic routines.

narrative_blocks.header: Gave liberally to Yale's Hundred Thousand Fund
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Cowles graduated Yale and later served as a 'liberal contributor' to the Hundred Thousand Dollar Fund raised by Yale alumni in support of the college. He earned the title 'Major Timothy' from Connecticut militia service that followed him into family records.

institutions.institution_blurb: Member of the Yale Corporation 1835; contributed to Yale's $100,000 fund.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Cowles cousins sheltered Mende girls in 1841
cross_connections.description: When the freed Amistad Mende were brought to Farmington in 1841, Timothy Cowles and his cousin Horace Cowles each took in one of the three surviving Mende girls. Timothy is believed to have sheltered Kagne; Horace sheltered Teme.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Farmington households that sheltered Mende girls in 1841
cross_connections.description: During the Amistad survivors' 1841 Farmington stay, Rev. Noah Porter Jr.'s family took in Margru while Timothy Cowles sheltered the girl believed to be Kagne. Both families were anchors of the Farmington abolitionist network that received the freed Mende.

cross_connections.display_label: Williams brought the Mende to Farmington — Cowles took one in
cross_connections.description: Austin Williams was one of the Farmington abolitionists who organized the freed Mende's relocation to Farmington after the 1841 Supreme Court ruling. Timothy Cowles was among the families who answered Williams's call by sheltering a Mende child.

cross_connections.display_label: Held the Mende during trial — Cowles housed one after release
cross_connections.description: Norris Willcox held the Mende captives in federal custody during their trial. After the Supreme Court freed them in March 1841, the Mende were brought to Farmington, where Major Timothy Cowles was among the families who sheltered one of the freed Mende girls.

cross_connections.display_label: Married his fourth cousin — both descend Rev. Samuel Hooker.

military_service.notes: Quartermaster 1808-9; ensign 1809; lt. Grenadier co. 1811; captain 1812-14; major 1815; major 14th CT Militia 1816-17.
Got the title 'Major Timothy' through service in the Connecticut militia. The title stuck in family records and EH 1909.

career.notes: War of 1812 service.
Regimental number changed from 15th to 14th.
Several years.
Several terms.
Liberal contributor to the $100,000 fund for the assistance of the college.

career.title: Merchant and farmer
career.notes: Merchant and farmer at Farmington CT per EH #698.

career.title: Connecticut State Senator
career.notes: Served as State Senator per EH #698.

education.notes: Yale graduate per EH #698. Specific year not documented in EH text.

sources.short: Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #1837 Jennette Cowles Williams

notable.notable_blurb: Documented Farmington abolitionist alongside her husband Austin F
notable.primary_url_label: NPS — Austin F. Williams Carriagehouse and House

cross_connections.display_label: Amistad defender from another Hooker branch — separate antislavery work

naming_inspiration.description: Likely named for her deceased aunt Jennette Deming (HD3663), Diadema's daughter who died in childhood.

career.title: Abolitionist
career.notes: Documented Farmington abolitionist (per NPS National Historic Landmark source). Worked alongside her husband Austin F. Williams in hosting the Amistad Mende captives at the family carriagehouse during 1841 and in the family's broader Underground Railroad activity.

narrative_blocks.header: Hosted the Amistad Mende captives in 1841
narrative_blocks.body: She and her husband Austin Franklin Williams hosted the Amistad Mende captives in their newly-built Farmington carriagehouse — completed 4 May 1841 — during the Africans' eight-month stay between Supreme Court victory and return to Sierra Leone. The carriagehouse and house are now a National Park Service-listed property.

narrative_blocks.header: A 'known abolitionist' alongside her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Jennette Cowles Williams was, in the words of the National Park Service, a 'known abolitionist' — working alongside her husband Austin F. Williams in the Farmington Amistad and Underground Railroad networks. The couple hosted the Mende captives in the carriagehouse on their property from May to November 1841 before the Africans returned to Sierra Leone.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost both first children in infancy
narrative_blocks.body: Her first two children — Eliza Cowles Williams and Horace Cowles Williams — died in infancy in 1832 and 1835 respectively. Her two surviving daughters Anna Maria and Catherine Deming both married and continued the Farmington Williams line into the next generation.

parents.father_research_notes: Ozias Williams of East Hartford CT
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Smith Williams of East Hartford CT

notable.notable_blurb: Farmington Connecticut abolitionist and member of the Amistad
notable.primary_url_label: NPS — Austin F. Williams Carriagehouse and House

cross_connections.display_label: Brought the Mende to Farmington — Cowles took one in
cross_connections.description: Austin Williams was one of the Farmington abolitionists who organized the freed Mende's relocation to Farmington after the 1841 Supreme Court ruling. Major Timothy Cowles was among the families who answered Williams's call by sheltering a Mende child.

cross_connections.display_label: Fellow Farmington abolitionist and Underground Railroad partner.
Fellow Connecticut abolitionist working through law and Underground Railroad.
Fellow abolitionist + Underground Railroad operator across the Connecticut-Ohio axis.
formed the committee that funded the Amistad defense

career.title: Drug store clerk and partner
career.notes: Began as a clerk in the drug store of Gen. George Cowles (HD0512) in Farmington CT, then was made a partner; firm name became Cowles & Williams.

career.title: Co-founder
career.notes: Organized the firm of Williams, Camp & Abbe in 1836 and opened a wholesale dry-goods store in New York City.

career.title: Stone and lumber yard owner
career.notes: Started a stone and lumber yard in Plainville, Connecticut.

career.title: Church choir leader
career.notes: Leader of the church choir in Farmington for many years.

career.title: Member, Amistad Defense Committee
career.notes: Member of the defense committee that worked to secure freedom for the Amistad Mende captives. Corresponded with Lewis Tappan, a fellow committee member. With Samuel Deming and John Treadwell Norton, arranged for the Africans to be sheltered in Farmington after their March 9, 1841 Supreme Court release.

career.title: Underground Railroad conductor
career.notes: Oral tradition documents Williams as an Underground Railroad conductor, helping make Farmington a major stop on the network.

career.title: Director, Freedman's Bureau of New England and New York
career.organization: Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands (Freedman's Bureau)
career.notes: Appointed Director of the Freedman's Bureau of New England and New York after the Civil War. Found housing and job opportunities for newly-freed African Americans.

narrative_blocks.header: Built the Amistad dormitory in 1841
narrative_blocks.body: On May 4, 1841 in a communal barn-raising, Austin Williams erected a temporary dormitory on his Farmington property to house the Mende captives recently freed by the Supreme Court Amistad ruling. The Mende men lived there for the rest of their eight-month stay in Farmington. After they sailed for Sierra Leone in November 1841, Williams converted the dormitory into a carriagehouse — today a National Historic Landmark.

narrative_blocks.header: Corresponded with Lewis Tappan on the Amistad defense
narrative_blocks.body: Williams was a member of the Amistad Defense Committee and corresponded throughout the imprisonment of the Mende with his friend Lewis Tappan, the prominent New York abolitionist and fellow committee member. Together with Farmington neighbors Samuel Deming and John Treadwell Norton, he arranged for the Africans to come to Farmington after their March 1841 release from prison.

narrative_blocks.header: Freedman's Bureau Director, New England and New York
narrative_blocks.body: After the Civil War, Williams was appointed Director of the Freedman's Bureau of New England and New York. In this role he located housing and job opportunities for newly-freed African Americans across his region — extending the abolitionist work of his Amistad years into post-emancipation reconstruction.

narrative_blocks.header: Eighty years a Farmington choir leader
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond his abolitionist activity, Williams was the leader of the First Church of Christ choir in Farmington for many years. The church under Rev. Noah Porter was a center of Farmington abolitionist organizing — its members provided housing, clothing, and education to the Mende during their 1841 stay.

narrative_blocks.header: Eliza died young
narrative_blocks.body: Eliza died in 1832 at the age of one.

narrative_blocks.header: Horace died young
narrative_blocks.body: Horace died in 1835 at the age of one.

Yale Theological Seminary; pastor Plainville CT Jun 10, 1841-Apr 1843.

bio.bio_blurb: Farmington minister turned lifelong town officer

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale Class of 1834. Became a Congregational minister, but after his father Major Timothy's 1858 death he settled at the family homestead in Farmington.

narrative_blocks.header: His voice failed and ended his ministry
narrative_blocks.body: Yale-trained, he was ordained and took a Plainville pulpit — but within two years his voice gave out and he left preaching. He tried starch manufacturing in Buffalo, then settled back on the Farmington homestead and filled nearly every town office for decades.

parents.father_research_notes: Rufus Hoyt Seeley of New Canaan / South Norwalk CT

education.institution_blurb: Theological Seminary New York 1842; preached for a time.

naming_inspiration.description: Named 'Hooker' to honor Hooker family descent through grandmother Catherine Lucina Cowles and great-grandmother Lucina Hooker Cowles (H00309).

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Deming of Plainville CT (not built per Jalapeño). Per EH#614 + WikiTree.
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Deming of Plainville CT (not built per Jalapeño). Per EH#614 + WikiTree.

career.title: Merchant
career.notes: Active in the Farmington mercantile trade ~1780-1825 — buying goods at Hartford and New York, importing in his own vessels with cargo of West India and East India goods. Per Trumbull's Memorial History (1886): 'foremost in enterprise.'

career.title: Director of the Hartford Bank
career.notes: Active and influential director of the Hartford Bank per Trumbull's Memorial History (1886).

career.title: Director of a Middletown bank
career.organization: Middletown Bank (specific institution unspecified in source)
career.notes: Active and influential director of a Middletown bank per Trumbull's Memorial History (1886).

narrative_blocks.header: Held specie reserves to save Hartford Bank
narrative_blocks.body: During the War of 1812, all Connecticut banks except the Hartford Bank suspended specie payments. Chauncey held large reserves of hard currency in Farmington and produced them as needed to keep the Hartford Bank's credit intact. He served as a director of banks in both Hartford and Middletown.

narrative_blocks.header: Powdered queue, blue coat, gilt buttons
narrative_blocks.body: Chauncey Deming was a strong rider and dressed for it. He galloped along Farmington Street on a 'strong and elegant horse,' and sat in church in a powdered queue, bright blue coat, and gilt buttons — a sartorial holdover from the eighteenth century preserved into the 1820s.

sources.short: Memorial History of Hartford County, Connecticut (Trumbull ed., 1886) — Unionville chapter by James L. Cowles
Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #614 Diadema Cowles Deming
Memorial History of Hartford County, Connecticut (Trumbull ed., 1886) — Unionville chapter by James L. Cowles
Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #614 Diadema Cowles Deming

narrative_blocks.header: Five of eight children died as infants
narrative_blocks.body: She and Chauncey Deming had eight children between 1784 and 1794; five died as infants or young children. The pattern included two infants named Samuel — the second born after the first had died — and three unnamed infants. Only Catharine (b.1787) and the first Samuel survived to adulthood; only Catharine produced descendants.

narrative_blocks.header: Christmas Day birth in Farmington, 1756
narrative_blocks.body: She was born on Christmas Day 1756 at Farmington Hartford County CT — second child of James Cowles and Abigail Hooker Cowles. The birth occurred at the height of the French and Indian War, twenty years before Farmington would send its sons to the Revolutionary muster fields.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife of Farmington's wealthiest 1790-1825 merchant
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband Chauncey Deming was the dominant Farmington merchant of his generation, per Trumbull's 1886 Memorial History of Hartford County. She died at Farmington in September 1826 aged sixty-nine, fifteen months before Chauncey himself died in his seventieth year.

sources.short: Connecticut Town Marriage Records pre-1870 (Barbour Collection)
Find a Grave — Memento Mori Cemetery Farmington CT
Genealogy of the Cowles family in America (Cowles, 1929)
sources.notes: James E. Cowles is entry #118 in Cowles Genealogy.

marriages.spouse_name: Mary Bull Cowles (1736-1811) — built as X02856 in Stage 6Y-189 per new spouse-entry policy.

parents.mother_research_notes: Elizabeth Smith Cowles (1689-1767), Capt. Isaac Cowles's third wife, not built per Jalapeño Rule.

military_service.notes: Appointed Ensign May 1771; promoted Lieutenant May 1772 of the 1st Company / Trainband, 15th Connecticut Militia. Per Cowles Genealogy entry #118.

career.title: Inheritor of Capt. Isaac Cowles homestead
career.notes: Inherited from his father the place at the south end of Farmington Street (occupied around 1850 by James W. Cowles, Cowles Genealogy entry #743 — not built).

narrative_blocks.header: Brothers both married Hooker-side second cousins
narrative_blocks.body: James and his older brother Ezekiel both married women who were second cousins to each other through their Hooker line — Ezekiel to Martha Hooker in 1748, James to Abigail Hooker in 1753. The brothers' children grew up first cousins on the Cowles side and third cousins on the Hooker side.

narrative_blocks.header: Lieutenant promotion came same month wife died
narrative_blocks.body: He was promoted from Ensign to Lieutenant of the 1st Company Trainband 15th Connecticut Militia in May 1772 — the same month his wife Abigail Hooker Cowles died. The personal-and-public events coincided in the heaviest weeks of his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost first wife 1772, remarried widow Mary Bull
narrative_blocks.body: Eighteen months after Abigail Hooker Cowles's February 1772 death, he married Mary Bull on 15 November 1773 — widow of William Lewis. The new wife brought stepchildren from her first marriage into the Farmington Cowles household.

sources.short: Connecticut Town Marriage Records pre-1870 (Barbour Collection)
Find a Grave — Memento Mori Cemetery Farmington CT

burial.plot_notes: Find a Grave memorial — Memento Mori Cemetery

cross_connections.display_label: Her second cousin Martha married her husband's older brother Ezekiel.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-six leaving seven children
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail married James E. Cowles in August 1753 — same Farmington family network that produced her cousin Martha's marriage to James's older brother Ezekiel a decade earlier. She bore seven children between 1754 and 1771 and died at thirty-six in February 1772, eighteen months before her husband remarried Mary Bull.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her cousin married Cowles brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail and her second cousin Martha Hooker Cowles married brothers — Abigail to James E. Cowles in 1753, Martha to Ezekiel Cowles a decade earlier. Both Hooker cousins descended from Rev. Samuel Hooker through different sons; both Cowles brothers were sons of Capt. Isaac Cowles by his third wife Elizabeth Smith.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
